posts.name: M a r k K a p l a n
posts.cooked:
The discussion about Champagne Release Prices (Champagne Release Prices) drifted eventually into a discussion of aging Champagne under cork, rather than crown cap. I thought I’d start a new conversation about the topic, because it interests me and I think it worthy of discussion. I’m going to copy some of that discussion to get things started. If I’m wrong, there will be few if any replies below, so here goes…

Champagne Release Prices

Years back, I sat with Raphael and Vincent (and others) a couple times as he was contemplating the move from crown cap to cork. We tasted blind the range with the second fermentation under cork and crown. In most cases, it was unanimous that the cork wine was superior and when it wasn’t unanimous, it was a majority in favor of cork. For Bereche’s winemaking, cork was a better match (for other winemakers it might not be). It brought an extra dimension of texture and richness to the wines that made them more appealing to me. Cork also has its challenges with variability, workflow, and other areas at high volumes so it wasn’t used for the Brut Reserve, but I wish it was. Overall, I will say that Bereche’s winemaking is on the oxidative side of the spectrum. Reflet d’Antan is the most extreme example in the lineup, but the entire range has seen some releases where the wines swung much more to the oxidative side.

Champagne Release Prices

With the cork thing, Rafael spent half an hour explaining to us the benefits of cork and why they used them and the oxaditive benefits played a large part in his reasoning.

Champagne Release Prices

I know Raphael likes to talk about the oxygen impact on the wines from the cork. I don’t fully agree as I think his wines are already on the oxidative side prior to bottling. I can see how the cork can highlight some of the richer oxidative or oxidative leaning characteristics, but I think it is more complex than cork = oxygen exchange. For Raphael’s wines, I agree that cork is a better choice for his winemaking style which happens to be on the oxidative side.

I find Filaine’s Cuvee Speciale and DMY to be an interesting comparison. Vins clairs all aged in old casks and no malo. The cépages are also similar, as is dosage. CS is aged sur lattes under crown cap for two years, while DMY ages under cork for four. The wines are stylistically similar, with DMY richer, deeper, and less effervescent than CS. After a few years in the cellar, CS puts on a little weight and gains some depth. While aging narrows the gap between the two somewhat, CS never reaches the full flavor profile of DMY.

Why? Two extra years sur latte before disgorgement? Cork aging v crown cap aging? Something else?
posts.display_username: M a r k K a p l a n

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
the only thing better than multiple champagne threads is another champagne thread. this topic is fascinating!

(yes, champagneberserkers.com is available for purchase).

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
Mark,

I don’t think we understand cork enough to answer this question completely. You can get a crown cap with differing permeabilities and the ingress/egress is essentially linear; the cap is designed to perform with a set of specifications. Mytik or Mytik-like closures are the same and this is why most Champagne producers have no interest in Mytik for a second fermetnation - you can get a crown cap to perform the same and it delivers a better overall value. Every cork is unique and there isn’t an easy formula that is going to tell you how it is going to perform and even if there was it would be quite complex and definitely not linear over time.

As to Filaine, a couple extra years on the lees makes a huge difference especially for a wine that is not seeing extended lees aging. My feeling is that the cork has much less of an effect on the final product than the amount of time aging on the lees. But, I do feel that the cork aging will help highlight some richness and depth that is only possible with the longer lees aging of the DMY. There may not be enough to highlight in the Cuvee Speciale and the cork may not make much or enough of a difference. Unless Fabrice wants to make the Cuvee Speciale and DMY in both crown and cork varieties, we won’t know. I think this is a case where the cork aging is a great accompaniment to the main force of the longer lees aging. DMY is normally dosed 1 g/L lower so that also can affect things, but the lees aging is still the key IMO.

posts.name: M a r k K a p l a n
posts.cooked: Thanks, Brad. Makes sense. Are there any other producers who use crown caps on some of their wines and cork on others during aging on the lees?
posts.display_username: M a r k K a p l a n

posts.name: Jordan_Rock
posts.cooked: Twice the likelihood of the wine being corked?

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
Jordan,

Yes, you are correct. This is a concern and some producers have made the investment for testing to eliminate the possibility of TCA as much as they can, but this isn’t inexpensive and there are still other potential problems with corks outside of TCA. If you want consistency across all bottles, use crown caps or Mytik/Mytik-like closures at all stages of the Champagne closure process.

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
Mark,

There are plenty. DP, Dom Ruinart, Bollinger, Geoffroy, Etienne Calsac, and Lallier come to mind first, but there are a heck of a lot more. While some do it for a specific reason after testing, some do it just because it is trendy or to try something new. Most tend to use them on lower volume wines and/or wines that see more time on the lees. DP takes things to another level with their P2/P3 as they are cork aged and volumes are in the millions now.

reply_to_user.name: M a r k K a p l a n

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
Something we have observed especially over the last few years is how champagnes are becoming more drinkable at an early age. Agrapart in the grower scene was the master at making champagnes which were open to drinking from the start. How big a part does the cork play in this, it is actually difficult to know who use them and who does not as this is not necessarily infrmation that producers provide.

I think it comes down to which possibilities have the producers for natural oxygenation. How oxaditve a champagne is often a direct result of how much oxygen contact there is during the fermentation. Krug are the masters here. What follows is more a case of how best to oxygenate naturally during elevage and during the second fermentation. Agrapart uses barrels and stainless steel so you have the aerobic element and the anaerobic element. Combine this with as short a sojourn on the lees as possible and you have a pretty accessible champagne from the beginning, low dosage and the champagne is drinkable straight after degorgement. This does not imply it will not improve with aging.

Both Agrapart and Selosse have done a lot of reseach in to the shape of the yeast particles, they wanted flat rather than round forms to optimailise contact surface to the liquid. This means one can get more yeast aromas in a shorter space of time.

One has to understand with Agrapart, he is also going for optimal ripeness which plays a big part in champagnes being drinkable young.

A really interesting producer, who tries to emulate Krug is Tristan Hyest. He sells a lot of Pinot noir to Krug and with his own champagnes he wants to emulate them. Fermentation in barrel but rather elevage in stainless steel, elevage is in magnums under cork. I do not know if he does second feremtnation under cork but his champagnes strangely have the Krug feel to them. Critics have not latched on to him but the Italian market has and it is really difficultt o get his champagnes. his champagnes are very accessible from the beginning.

With sur lees aging I think one has to take the site into consideration. A really good example of this going wrong is with Elise Bougy, she inherited from her grand mother a site in Chetillons. Granted she had little financial resources, her first Chetillons release was a disaster and she took a lot of criticism for it. Producers said Chetillons especially need more time on the lees, it softens the champagne. One sees the same in Ay, the champagnes need more time on the lees . Paul Gosset learned this the hard way.

When one talks with producers, they often speak of the champagne not being ready and it needs another 6 months on the lees and then another 6 months on the lees. I think this has especially to do with the big sites and vintage champagnes.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: You can tell from the rim of the bottle (the “goulot”) whether it was tirage under crown cap or cork, Donald! So the information is hiding in plain sight! You need a thicker goulot with a different profile to be able to hold the staple that holds in the cork.

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked: As you need the ‘flared’ rim for the crown cap.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: One bit of historical context for this discussion is that the first generation of crown caps actually had composite cork liners. Which were very susceptible to TCA and also degraded over time. So you got the worst of both world. Egly for example had big issues pushing longer sur lattes with these capsules, and the wines ended up more oxidative back in the 1990s than they likely would have done under cork. He regrets not having disgorged all his old library wines that he kept under capsules for this reason. Once polymer lined crown caps became available, things changed very much for the better.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
As you make wine, I assume you ferment in the barrel as I believe most burgundian producers do. I feel as toasting regimes are being dialled back, we are seeing much more accessible wines from the start.

This is something I always wonder about in the Champagne. Few producers tend to ferment in the barrel, I find those that do tend to make more balanced champagnes than those who fill the wine into barrel after fermentation. Apart from Egly, three producers who really stand out at this for me are Savart, Leclapart and Agrapart. Although the discussion is about caps versus corks more in the context of Bereche, but I wonder personally how important fermentation is, as a whole for the later outcome.

Not really wanting to stir up a Ulysse Collin discussion, a lot of us see the likes of Selosse and Ulysse Collin as being a bit anachronostic. It fascinates me that numerous younger producers are not following there path rather rebelling against it. We always ask young producers, who has influenced them most and the answer is usually not what one would expect. My feeling is the Jura influence is becoming more pronounced in the Champagne, in terms of working with natural oxygenation.

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
William,

Yes, the entire premise of cork lining the inside of a crown cap is a great example of skewed thinking, but it went on for a long time; I recall still seeing these in the early 2000s even though some had moved away from them decades before that. Additionally, the first version of crown caps that came out in the mid to late 1960s had a high failure rate seal-wise (especially for extended aging) and the first folks that moved to crown caps ended up with wines that matured far too quickly or oxidized and had to be discarded. Clicquot was one of the first to move in the mid-1960s and got hit pretty hard with this.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Toasting is a big part of it, and a complex subject in itself as it comprises how hot, covered or not, how long, what sort of heat… Then there is grain, origin, seasoning… all of which is inter-related with toast, e.g. less seasoned wood with more toast is not the same as very seasoned wood with more toast. These variables then interact with the wine. There are principles but no rules, and it really takes in my so far very limited experience about 3-5 years to figure out what is ideal. As one gets more experience it gets easier, but not that much easier.

Fermenting in tank before going to barrel I don’t think inherently makes oakier-tasting wines, it’s just a matter of the barrels and the temperature of barreling down for the most part. The French have the expression, don’t feed strawberries to pigs; and in commerce, you tend to get what they think you deserve rather than what you ask for. I am not convinced that many producers in Champagne are getting the good barrels, and there are fewer and fewer to go around. Hydric stress in oak trees makes the wood taste of coconut, who knew. So… lots to think about! And as ever, no simple answers.

As for going in a vin nature/Jurassic direction, fine, it’s one route to escape the glass ceilings imposed by the traditional hierarchy of terroirs without necessarily having to do the work in the vineyards and the winery to make great wines. There’s a market for it, and I think today we are seeing a Balkanisation of the wine market, with lots of different niches, but happily without the need for those niches to attack each other. I do tend to think that gout de souris/brett/high VA Champagnes will struggle to find a broad audience, and they simply don’t interest me in any case. A very trendy US importer once said I have a Bourgeois palate, and without bearing it as a badge of honor it did not unduly perturb me.

posts.cooked:

There’s a market for it, and I think today we are seeing a Balkanisation of the wine market

I think this has made my day. I sell champagne etc and have to cater and understand different tastes, and often when one tastes new stuff one can get over excited and lose perspective.

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked:
William, I think most ‘natural’ (even worse adjective when talking about Champagne) producers would argue that it makes things harder in the vineyard!

Would you not agree?

I suppose it depends what we are comparing with.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I think it’s important to understand that natural winemaking is a winemaking movement in its origins, not an agronomic approach. There are plenty of “natural” producers who do not farm organically, and who purchase fruit from vineyards treated with herbicides.

I wrote an essay about the history of the natural wine movement in Noble Rot a year or two ago, I might ask them if I can republish it here.

Anyway, it’s quite a digression from the topic at hand!

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked:
We’ll then we are back to a definition problem.

For me ‘at least organic’ (allowing for also wanting to minimise copper) is necessary… (based on every attempt at a definition I recall seeing)

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: That is just the difference between what Anglophone observers think it “should” mean, and what the movement, given its historical origins going back to Chauvet and Neauport, actually represents in France.

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked:
Well not just anglophone. Several of the definitions are from French organisations.

I’m sure there’s a very interesting exercise in examining the origins but the practical one lives today.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: On the contrary, looking at its origins explain why plenty of iconic “natural” wineries don’t actually meet the various and contested attempted definitions of natural wine.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
I had a bit of time to delve into the subject a bit more deeper and spoke to a producer we work with. What strikes me is it being a case of semantics first and foremostly. I probably over use the word oxaditive as customers ask for either champagnes that or oxidative or not. And I tend to bracket producers in these categories. It is the same with minerality, customers understand what one means even though it is a vague term.

The presumption with aging under cork allowing the champagne to breathe and adds an oxaditve character comes in my case from a visit to Bereche. The concensus however seems to be aging under cork adds creaminess and additional complexity. I am a great admirer of Agrapart and the best phrase on oxadition I think comes from him “oxidation is not the enemy of wine.” Although I would not say Agrapart’s champagnes have an "oxidative feel to them, I feel he is a master at manipulating oxgen to make outstanding champagnes.

One can counter this with a quote from Lécaillon/Roederer - We hate oxidation - it is a betrayal of terroir." To go futher he says, oxidation creates savoury overtones that distract from fruit purity.

A house famous for aging under cork is Bollinger and I think alongside Agrapart they are the leaders in making young approachable champagnes, The absolute opposite of Bollinger is Ruinart, where they are doing everything possible to create a reductive champagne, uses of pneumatic press, inert gas, stainless steel.

To complicate matters Jacquesson use oak foudres to allow the wine to breathe. They say lees stirring has an antioxidant effect and produces a creamy effect.

Another interesting aspect is that during the second fermentation, alongside the autolysis oxidation also occurs, obviously less under crown caps than under corks, but it seems to be a part of the process. There is the presumption longer lees aging leads to complexer champagnes, no question this is true. Yet on the other side, new grower producers need their champagnes to be drunk young and with two producers in particular I have seen massive jumps in quality and complexity despite short lees aging. Adren Renoir in particular comes to mind. The 18s I felt showed potential but there was room for improvement. The 19s however “oxaditive” in character are some of the complexest champagnes I have tasted last year and this sort of contradicts as does Agrapart’s champagnes that long lees aging is necessary for complex champagnes.

On saturday I was talking with a colleague about oxidation and Bereche, I think the first thought was, we both do not really pick up oxidative notes in champagne, drunk too much. Even my wife, who is more a passive passenger, when it comes to champagne used too hate oxidative wines from Jura but now she does not mind.

As I said above, customers tend to use descriptors that are very imprecise, I want an oxaditve champagne, or I want brioche. I know what they mean and probably tend to use these terms more freely myself, when a more precise language is required but that is entering into the realms of geekdom and will customers understand.

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
D_Pennet:

A house famous for aging under cork is Bollinger and I think alongside Agrapart they are the leaders in making young approachable champagnes, The absolute opposite of Bollinger is Ruinart, where they are doing everything possible to create a reductive champagne, uses of pneumatic press, inert gas, stainless steel.

Donald - Ruinart is definitely reductive, but is also now using cork on regular release Dom Ruinart BdB and had already been using it on the La Reserve Dom Ruinart. How does this fit in with your statements?

posts.name: M a r k K a p l a n
posts.cooked:
This is one of the reasons why I find Champagne to be so fascinating. To me, it often seems more like alchemy than wine making.

Does it matter when oxygen is introduced to the wine? Is there a different effect if the wine is subject to oxygen in barrel/tank than in bottles?
posts.display_username: M a r k K a p l a n

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
M.Kaplan:

Does it matter when oxygen is introduced to the wine? Is there a different effect if the wine is subject to oxygen in barrel/tank than in bottles?

It can have quite a dramatic effect (especially when variables such as keeping oxygen away from the wine starting from the crush / letting the must oxygenate thoroughly prior to fermentation, but protecting the wine from oxygen at that point / aging the wine oxidatively in oak barrels / introduction of oxygen in bottle aging).

I’ve understood the impact of oxygen in bottle aging prior to disgorgement is the smallest, because so very little oxygen makes its way into the bottle and the yeast lees from the liqueur de tirage create a reductive environment in the bottle, binding a good amount of free oxygen in the process.

However, the impact the oxygen can make in the bottle is bigger if the wine hasn’t seen any oxygen prior to aging sur lattes. If the wine has been aged completely reductively prior to bottling, there are more compounds that the introduced oxygen can oxidize ie. change in the wine compared to a wine which has been aged oxidatively, making it more protected against oxygen later in the process. This is why the choice of closure during the aging process might have a different impact on the wine depending entirely on the cuvée and the style the producer is making their wines in.

reply_to_user.name: M a r k K a p l a n

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
Otto_Forsberg:
M.Kaplan:

Does it matter when oxygen is introduced to the wine? Is there a different effect if the wine is subject to oxygen in barrel/tank than in bottles?

It can have quite a dramatic effect (especially when variables such as keeping oxygen away from the wine starting from the crush / letting the must oxygenate thoroughly prior to fermentation, but protecting the wine from oxygen at that point / aging the wine oxidatively in oak barrels / introduction of oxygen in bottle aging).

Otto,
I know this has been mentioned in past threads, but I continue to be fascinated by the use of oxidizing juice prior to fermentation especially by reductive producers. Since Moet first talked of doing this with their 2003 vintage wines, it has started to be used more and more especially in the warmer, lower acid, high phenolic years such as 2022. The fact that most folks only tend to let the Pinot Noir juice oxidize, but in years like 2003 and 2022 some did it on both Pinot Noir and Chardonnay just shows how many tools the winemaker has and that nothing is necessarily easy or straightforward. A number of reductive producers are having a lot of success with allowing juice to oxidze and then rabidly protecting the wine from Oxygen, even with the use of a cork during second fermentation. DP has gotten to the point where they are continuously tracking Oxygen in the wine at every point from press to disgorgement.

*Forgot to add in “and Chardonnay” after “…on both Pinot Noir” in sentence 3

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Brad_Baker:

Otto,
I know this has been mentioned in past threads, but I continue to be fascinated by the use of oxidizing juice prior to fermentation especially by reductive producers. Since Moet first talked of doing this with their 2003 vintage wines, it has started to be used more and more especially in the warmer, lower acid, high phenolic years such as 2022. The fact that most folks only tend to let the Pinot Noir juice oxidize, but in years like 2003 and 2022 some did it on both Pinot Noir just shows how many tools the winemaker has and that nothing is necessarily easy or straightforward. A number of reductive producers are having a lot of success with allowing juice to oxidze and then rabidly protecting the wine from Oxygen, even with the use of a cork during second fermentation. DP has gotten to the point where they are continuously tracking Oxygen in the wine at every point from press to disgorgement.

Indeed, the higher the phenolic concentration is, the more there is something to oxidize in the wine. By letting these easily oxidized phenolics oxidize and drop out of solution, the wine loses most of the stuff that could go south later on in the process. And, after all, the grape must needs a good dose of oxygen for the yeast to get going!

As fermentation depletes most of the oxygen from the must / wine, it’s easy to make wines very reductively by limiting oxygen ingress after that point.

I guess they have started to oxidize the Chardonnay must prior to fermentation might’ve started after learning some esteemed producers employ the very same process with their white wines in places like Burgundy, Mosel and Nahe. Well, I don’t know if the Champagne producers have ever even heard of any wine regions beyond the French borders, but I guess they still know Burgundy!

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
Otto,

Champagne definitely likes to talk about Burgundy (and they say Burgundy likes to equally talk about Champagne). A number of Champagne winemakers simplified view of premox has always been that Burgundy historically has been a land full of red winemakers. They made their whites as if they were red wines and it worked well for long aging and improvement. When they started to be a bit too gentle and make a white wine with a different, let’s say lighter touch, bad things sometimes happened. Champagne which has always been a wine made with a lighter touch (you need around 22% more grapes to get 1 hL of juice compared to Burgundy) is learning to adjust to years where the wines are going to be higher in Phenolics in the Pinots and sometimes the Chardonnays. In these types of years, they have learned from Burgundy (and others too). This is a simplification, but it gets the quick view of some in Champagne across.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
Regardless if the fermentation/elevage is conducted in a oxaditve or reductive nature, the cork releases due to the pressure implemented on it when pressed into the bottle 2,5 to 3 milligrams of oxygen which is enough to cause oxidative aging, desired or undesired. This effect is the same with all types of corks.

Jetting in this case cannot prevent this. There are developments to create corks which are inert, by soaking them for some considerable time in ovepressurised nitrogen. It is the belief, the combination of jetting and inert corks would prevent any oxygen getting into the champagne and would make adding sulphur at bottling unnecessary.

and they say Burgundy likes to equally talk about Champagne

One of the good things in Burgundy is their not taking champagne seriously, which results in the restaurants having really good prices for top producer’s bottlings.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
D_Pennet:

Regardless if the fermentation/elevage is conducted in a oxaditve or reductive nature, the cork releases due to the pressure implemented on it when pressed into the bottle 2,5 to 3 milligrams of oxygen which is enough to cause oxidative aging, desired or undesired. This effect is the same with all types of corks.

That is true, but the effect this small dose of O2 can be wildly different depending on how the wine was brought up. An oxidatively made wine might show very little change, whereas a very reductively made Champagne could change more drastically and at a faster rate.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
I can understand the difference this would make to a reductive champagne. And no question the effect on oxidative champagne may not be so pronounced. I think looking at a producer like Agrapart, he does not miss any oppurtunity to microoxigenate, considering how open the Mineral, Avizoise and the Venus are post degorgement, obviously low dosage and undergoing malolactic play a large part here. Yet Bereche blocks the malolactic and we know they are not averse to higher dosages as they see this as a way for long aging.

William Kelley speaks of producers in Champagne having difficulty obtaining high quality barrels, this may be true of new barrels, but Agrapart buys used five year old barrels from the Loire and Burgundy, explicitly saying he wants them not for woody aromas but for oxidisation. This is the same of Egly, who also buys used barrels.

This is more anecdotal. About five years ago I was at a tasting where the majority of the producers worked in an oxidative manner, I knew about 80% of the champagnes from previous releases. On the basis of that particular tasting my hierarchy was, the top two Agrapart and Bereche, then Pouillon, Emanuel Brochet and Marie Courtin beneath them. I remember Pacal Doquet’s champagnes not performing well at all which really surprised me at the time.
This tasting really bothered me and over the course of the next six months as the new releases arrived I tasted the champagnes that left me feeling underwhelmend at that tasting. The conclusions drawn from that tasting were contradicted again and again. I am not saying Bereche or Agrapart are not top producers but such tastings obviously favour producers who have mastered the art of making oxidative champagnes.

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
D_Pennet:

Regardless if the fermentation/elevage is conducted in a oxaditve or reductive nature, the cork releases due to the pressure implemented on it when pressed into the bottle 2,5 to 3 milligrams of oxygen which is enough to cause oxidative aging, desired or undesired. This effect is the same with all types of corks.

Jetting in this case cannot prevent this. There are developments to create corks which are inert, by soaking them for some considerable time in ovepressurised nitrogen. It is the belief, the combination of jetting and inert corks would prevent any oxygen getting into the champagne and would make adding sulphur at bottling unnecessary.

Donald,

Are folks jetting at bottling (I don’t think this is technically possible) and does any Oxygen present in the cork matter at this point in the process? There is a second fermentation that is going to take place so I would expect any oxygen that is present to get consumed by the yeast. I think the concern/question is more on Oxygen transfer after the second fermentation is complete and that is where I don’t think enough is understood in regards to crown cap vs. cork and then you also have the way you are storing the bottles ‘sur latte’ and/or ‘sur pointe’ and when as the dead yeast cells can create an additional barrier if they accumulate at the closure.

I do agree with you on very reasonably priced bottles of Champagne in Burgundy. Likewise, in Champagne, you can find some pretty good Burgundy deals at restaurants as well (at least compared to US pricing).

*Edited first line by adding in ‘(I don’t think this is technically possible)’

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
Jetting is always post degorgement. The contact with oxygen seems to be more of an obsession with the maisons rather than the growers. Some one posted here a link to a good article on jetting but I cannot find it.

I don’t think anyone stores bottles sur point, this is the last phase of riddling.

link_counts.title: IOC - Jetting lors du dégorgement - YouTube

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
Donald,

Thanks, yes, I know what Jetting is. As for a good article, you may have been referring to this one that was written by Tom Stevenson for World of Fine Wine: Jetting in Champagne

My confusion is that we are talking about using cork for the second fermentation closure which is prior to disgorgement. The dynamics of evolution post disgorgement are very different than those during the second fermentation period.

As for aging sur pointe, this is getting more attention and research. The most well known producers now using it for some of their longer aging prestige releases are Louis Roederer and Ruinart. For those that age this way, I have seem them start sur latte for the autolysis period, riddle, and move to sur pointe for a number of years before disgorgement. With the yeast accumulating at the closure (whether crown cap or cork), you get an additional layer of protection against gaseous exchanges such as Oxygen.

Ployez-Jacquemart also ages sur pointe. A small explanation here:

Bowler Wine

Ployez-Jacquemart

Laurence Ployez is a third-generation winemaker at her family's estate, which was established in 1930. Ployez-Jacquemart owns 2.15 hectares of Premier Cru and Grand Cru vineyards in Ludes and Mailly-Champagne in the Montagne de Reims, known for its...

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
Brad_Baker:

Are folks jetting at bottling (I don’t think this is technically possible) and does any Oxygen present in the cork matter at this point in the process? There is a second fermentation that is going to take place so I would expect any oxygen that is present to get consumed by the yeast. I think the concern/question is more on Oxygen transfer after the second fermentation is complete and that is where I don’t think enough is understood in regards to crown cap vs. cork and then you also have the way you are storing the bottles ‘sur latte’ and/or ‘sur pointe’ and when as the dead yeast cells can create an additional barrier if they accumulate at the closure.

Coming back to this statement.

Are folks jetting at bottling (I don’t think this is technically possible) and does any Oxygen present in the cork matter at this point in the process? There is a second fermentation that is going to take place so I would expect any oxygen that is present to get consumed by the yeast. I think the concern/question is more on Oxygen transfer after the second fermentation is complete and that is where I don’t think enough is understood in regards to crown cap vs. cork and then you also have the way you are storing the bottles ‘sur latte’ and/or ‘sur pointe’ and when as the dead yeast cells can create an additional barrier if they accumulate at the closure.

I am in the grower scene active and superficially taste now and again in the maisons. I don’t want to make general statements yet is seems most grower producers are relaxed about contact with oxygen. However with Ruinart as an example, they seem to see any contact with oxygen as a failure and are actively pursuing an absolute reductive style champagne.

This is a personal observation. With the young producers we work with, we have seen massive differences in the releases over the last three years with champagnes becoming more and more accessible post degorgement. What interests me, how does one achieve this? Aging under cork plays a part in this, how big I cannot not say, one would really have to taste before tirage and then post degorgement to evaluate this.

Also the aromatic differences in champagnes between the second fermentation and the third aging process post degorgement seem to me not to be so pronounced if dosage is held low and malolactic has occured. How will these champagnes age long term or are they even suited for long term aging?

I have many customers, who do not like the patina of age in their champagnes and who drink champagne way to young for my liking, I feel prodcuers recognise this and are making champagnes that cater to such a public.

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
D_Pennet:

I am in the grower scene active and superficially taste now and again in the maisons. I don’t want to make general statements yet is seems most grower producers are relaxed about contact with oxygen. However with Ruinart as an example, they seem to see any contact with oxygen as a failure and are actively pursuing an absolute reductive style champagne.

Donald,

But you can have Oxygen exchange and still be reductive. Ruinart isn’t trying to prevent all contact with Oxygen. They are using cork during the second fermentation process of Dom Ruinart and still producing a very reductive Champagne. They have done numerous studies comparing the seal of cork to crown cap and the results are very interesting especially for Champagnes you are going to age for 5-10+ years before disgorgement.

I am probably sounding like a broken record, but my point is that things aren’t so simple and the use of a cork during second fermentation doesn’t mean oxidative or non-reductive. There is way more to it than that. The use of the cork may highlight certain aspects of a wine in a positive or negative way, but it isn’t going to turn a wine oxidative or mean the winemaking is not reductive.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
I think we are both on the same page. I don’t really want to use the word oxidative as it has certian connations which can cause misunderstanding.

What are Ruinart’s motives for using a cork during the second fermentation? My guess would be to make the champagne more accessible from a young age. Perhaps you can elaborate on their motives.

I wrote before, Rafael Bereche explained to us in great detail how he saw the cork during the second fermentation as an art miniature barrique barrel which allowed the champagne to breathe.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked:
LVMH marketing dollars at work here:

According to Fred, the cork lets less oxygen in than the cap over time, so it actually produces a more reductive Champagne.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
I managed to talk to a producer, who ages under cork about this. Their feeling is we are still very much at the beginning of understanding what happens but the feeling is champagnes aged under cork are complexer and more balanced than champagnes aged under a metal cap.

The biggest issue is price.

The amount of oxygen a cork lets in is minimal but the problem is, the secondary fermentation is reductive in that the yeast devours the oxygen in the bottle which can result in unwanted reductive aromas if a metal cap is used. Although aging under cork is reductive, the champagnes are felt to be more balanced aged under cork than those aged under a metal cap.

There is also a feeling that with longer tirages, champagnes age better under cork than under crown caps.
